- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

6	This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using

9	bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention

17	## Cloning
18	
19	The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
20	makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
21	clone both when doing the initial clone:
22	```
23	git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1

26	If you forget to use --recurisze you need to do two additional

37	git submodule update --init

40	## jj Tips for Git Users

58	jj git push

196	### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`
197	
198	If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
199	tools like `gitk --all`.
200	
201	jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
202	When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
203	jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
204	undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
205	shows the current version of each change.
206	
207	### Useful commands
208	
209	| Command | Description |
210	|---------|-------------|
211	| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
212	| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
213	| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
214	| `jj st | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
215	| `jj st -r <chid> | Status of the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
216	| `jj show | Show the Working commit, -r @ |
217	| `jj show -r <chid> | Show the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
218	| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
219	| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |
220	
221	
222	In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
223	the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.
224	
225	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
226	
227	Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
228	to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
229	(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
230	and jj changeID:
231	
232	```
233	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
234	ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
235	```
236	
237	Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
238	Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
239	within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.
240	
241	For full details see:
242	- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
243	  — [ochid (Other Change ID)](./notes/chores-01.md#ochid-other-change-id)
244	  — [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
245	  — [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)
246	
247	## Contributing
248	
249	Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
250	canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):
251	
252	- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
253	  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
254	- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
255	- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
256	  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
257	- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
258	  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
259	  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
260	- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).
261	
262	Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
263	near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
264	details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
265	rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

# actor-x1

An experiment in creating an actor model in rust
see [notes/design.md](notes/design.md) for details.

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## Cloning

The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
clone both when doing the initial clone:
```
git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
```

If you forget to use --recurisze you need to do two additional
steps "init" and "update":
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule init
git submodule update
```

And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule update --init
```

## jj Tips for Git Users

See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).

### Initial Commit for a repo

Create create directory add files.

Minimal commands to push 

```
jj git init .
jj describe
jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
jj bookmark create main -r @
jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
jj git push
```

### Push a change to main

Assuming that this is to be push to main you
set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
then just push:

```
jj bookmark set main -r @
jj git push
```

### Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`

If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
tools like `gitk --all`.

jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
shows the current version of each change.

### Useful commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
| `jj st | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
| `jj st -r <chid> | Status of the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj show | Show the Working commit, -r @ |
| `jj show -r <chid> | Show the commit, <chid> such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |


In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
and jj changeID:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
```

Paths always start with `/` (the workspace root, i.e. vc-x1).
Each repo has a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its location
within the workspace, so tools can resolve these paths locally.

For full details see:
- [Git trailer convention](./notes/chores-01.md#git-trailer-convention)
  — [ochid (Other Change ID)](./notes/chores-01.md#ochid-other-change-id)
  — [ChangeID path syntax](./notes/chores-01.md#changeid-path-syntax)
  — [.vc-config.toml](./notes/chores-01.md#vc-configtoml)

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

# Notes

See [design.md](design.md)


## jj tips

For users new to jj see [jj-tips.md](jj-tips.md).

```
## Chores format

Filename: "chores-XX.md"
example: chores-01.md

Format of section labels: "## <short description> (X.Y.Z)"
example: "## Topic format description (0.1.0)"

Example chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Do something (1.3.1)

Describe something
```

## Versioning during development

This is using jujustiu, jj + git and we'll see how it goes. Below is my
git workflow, jj will be different but we'll have to discover that as
we go.

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump directly to
  `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-devN`, implement
  across multiple commits, final commit removes `-devN`.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before starting.

For multi-step:
1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-devN` with a plan and commit as a chore marker
2. Implement in one or more `-devN` commits (bump N as needed)
3. Final commit removes `-devN`, updates todo/chores — this is the "done" marker

The final release commit (without `-devN`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits. This keeps the git history readable and makes it easy to see which
commits were exploratory vs final.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo

- Add new feature X [details](chores-01.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done

- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: chores-01bugs.md#bug-y

# actor-x1

## Overview

Actor-x1 is a system that uses Communicating
Sequential Process (CSP) invented by Tony Hoare
In 1978 and is commonly termed the actor model.

 - There will be a management system (MS) that:

   - Provides the capability to interact with
     the runtime providing control and to capture
     logs and assists in developing the app
   - Creates an hello world scaffolding
   - provides discovery capabilities of other
     other actors.

 - A runtime that drives the system and provides
   resources and services for the system
   - interfaces between the manager and the application
 
 - One or more actors running on one or more
   threads. Each actor adheres to these
   invariants:
   - Has on basic entry point; handler()
   - The handler() handles one message at a time and
     must run to completion as quickly as possible.
   - The handler() never ever blocks
   - If a handler() has tasks that take to much time
     a technique that can be used is to spawn
     threads to do work. That thread would then use
     messages to communicate with its parent and
     other actors as it does its work.

## Future capabilities
   - I would like Actors to be implemented as an HSM.
     This potentially allows an Actor to implement
     sophisticated algorithms and prove correct operation.

## Stage1 runtime

Design and implement the simplest runtime possible

### Message

struct Message {}

### Actor

Actor implements an Actor trait

trait Actor {
  // Handles a message 
  fn handle_message(msg: Message)

  // other methods..
}

### Goal1

- Run two actors on one thread.
- A single message which is an empty struct
- Message ping pongs between the two actors
  for duration in seconds specified as a f64
  in seconds from the command line

## Goal2

- Same as Goal1 but each actor on a separate thread.

## Stage2 runtime

We will design and implement the slightly more capable runtime

It supports 3 actors A, B and C

### Message

struct Message {
  src_id: u32,
  dst_id: u32,
  send_count: u64,
}

### Actor

Actor implements an Actor trait

trait Actor {
  // Create a new actor
  fn new(id: u32, name: &str) -> Self;

  // Return the actors name
  fn name(&Self) -> &str;

  // Handle a message
  fn handle_message(msg: mut Message);

  // other methods..
}

### Goal1

- Run 3 actors on one thread.
- Runtime "sends" 5 messages each to a random destination actor
- Each actor forwards the message they receive to a random
  dst_id that they select.

### Goal2
- Same as Goal1 but each actor on a separate thread.

content.text:
Acquainted. Summary of what I found:

**Project**: `actor-x1` — a Rust experiment in the actor model (CSP, Hoare 1978). Currently design-only; no source code yet.

**Design stages** (notes/design.md):
- **Stage1**: minimal runtime — 2 actors ping-pong a unit `Message`; Goal1 single-thread, Goal2 per-thread.
- **Stage2**: 3 actors (A/B/C), `Message { src_id, dst_id, send_count }`, random forwarding.
- **Future**: actors as HSMs.

**Todo** (notes/todo.md): Initial design, PoC runtime, PoC manager — nothing in progress.

**Repo layout**: dual-repo — app repo at `.`, bot session repo at `.claude/` (git submodule + jj). Cross-linked via `ochid:` commit trailers.

**Conventions I'll follow** (CLAUDE.md):
- Conventional Commits with trailing `(X.Y.Z)`; every plan starts with a version bump (single-step or `X.Y.Z-0`/`-1`/… multi-step).
- Two-checkpoint Commit → Push+Finalize flow per step, with explicit approval at each checkpoint; hard stop after finalize.
- `//!` module doc + `///` on every fn/method; `// OK: …` justification on every non-test `.unwrap*`.
- Ask on ambiguity; don't use the memory dir (CLAUDE.md is canonical).
- "The bot thinks …" prefix for speculation in durable text.
- Last recent commit: `6029a15 docs: Update CLAUDE and README to latest version`.

Ready for direction.

## Stage 1 implementation proposal

**Recommended approach**: multi-step, `0.1.0-0 → 0.1.0-1 → 0.1.0-2 → 0.1.0`. Stage 1 bundles three discrete pieces (bootstrap, Goal1, Goal2), each non-trivial. One-step would work but multi-step gives a clean per-goal checkpoint. Happy to flip to single-step per-goal (`0.1.0`, `0.2.0`) if you'd rather see each Goal as its own "done" marker.

**Scaffolding (0.1.0-0, chore marker, no behavior)**
- `cargo init --lib` at repo root → crate named `actor_x1`.
- `src/lib.rs` — re-exports `runtime` and `actor` modules.
- `src/actor.rs` — `Message`, `Actor` trait.
- `src/runtime.rs` — single-threaded and threaded runtimes.
- `src/bin/goal1.rs`, `src/bin/goal2.rs` — each takes one f64 positional arg (duration seconds). Start with hand-rolled `std::env::args` parsing; add `clap` only if we grow.
- `Cargo.toml` version = `0.1.0-0`.

**Goal1 — single thread, 2 actors (0.1.0-1)**
- `struct Message;` (unit per spec).
- `trait Actor { fn handle_message(&mut self, ctx: &mut Context, msg: Message); }` — **deviates slightly from the design sketch** (adds `&mut self` and a `Context`). See open question below.
- `Context::send(&mut self, dst_id: u32, msg: Message)` pushes onto a runtime queue.
- `Runtime` owns `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(dst_id, Message)>`. Loop: pop → dispatch → repeat until `Instant::now() >= deadline`. Seed with one message from actor 0 → actor 1. Report total messages handled.

**Goal2 — one thread per actor (0.1.0-2)**
- Each actor owns a `mpsc::Receiver<Message>`; every actor holds a `Vec<Sender<Message>>` indexed by `dst_id` (or a `HashMap`).
- Spawn a thread per actor: `while let Ok(msg) = rx.recv() { actor.handle_message(msg); }`.
- Seed: main sends one `Message` into actor 0's channel, sleeps `duration`, then drops all senders. `recv()` errors → threads exit cleanly → `join`.
- No shared mutable state beyond the channels; each actor's state is thread-local.

**Final (0.1.0)** — drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` (move 'PoC runtime' to Done), add `notes/chores-01.md` section "Stage 1 runtime (0.1.0)".

**Open question I want to resolve before coding**

The design's trait signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has no `&self` and no way to emit a reply. With an empty `Message {}`, ping-pong requires *some* way for a handler to trigger the next send. My proposal adds `&mut self` + a `Context` handle (option b). Alternatives:
- (a) `fn handle_message(&mut self, msg: Message) -> Option<(u32, Message)>` — handler returns the next send. Simpler; less extensible.
- (c) Keep the signature literal and drive ping-pong from the runtime loop itself (actors become no-ops) — matches spec but isn't really an actor.

**The bot thinks** (b) is the right call because Stage 2 will need a multi-target send anyway, so starting there avoids a churn rewrite. But this is a design decision — want to confirm (b), or pick (a) or (c)?

1	//! Hardware tick counter abstraction: thin wrapper over the
2	//! target architecture's fixed-rate monotonic counter.
3	//!
4	//! Probes call three functions; the per-arch impl lives in a
5	//! child module gated by `#[cfg(target_arch = ...)]`:
6	//!
7	//! - [`read_ticks`] — current counter value.
8	//! - [`ticks_per_ns`] — calibrated conversion ratio.
9	//! - [`require_ok`] — exit the process if the counter isn't
10	//!   usable for probe measurements.
11	//!
12	//! Only `x86_64` is implemented today. AArch64 (`CNTVCT_EL0`)
13	//! and RISC-V (`time` CSR) both have architecturally invariant
14	//! counters by ISA spec, so their `require_ok` will be a no-op
15	//! once those impls land.
16	
17	#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
18	mod x86_64;
19	
20	#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
21	use x86_64 as imp;
22	
23	#[cfg(not(target_arch = "x86_64"))]
24	compile_error!(
25	    "iiac-perf probes currently only support target_arch = \"x86_64\". \
26	     Add a per-arch impl module (AArch64: CNTVCT_EL0; RISC-V: time CSR) \
27	     and wire it into src/ticks.rs."
28	);
29	
30	/// Read the current tick counter. Monotonic and fixed-rate.
31	#[inline(always)]
32	pub fn read_ticks() -> u64 {
33	    imp::read_ticks()
34	}
35	
36	/// Calibrated conversion ratio: counter ticks per nanosecond.
37	/// Cached — the first call does the work.
38	pub fn ticks_per_ns() -> f64 {
39	    imp::ticks_per_ns()
40	}
41	
42	/// Verify the tick counter is usable for probe measurements;
43	/// exit the process (code 1) with a diagnostic if not. The
44	/// checks performed depend on the target architecture.
45	pub fn require_ok() {
46	    imp::require_ok();
47	}

## Addendum — perf measurement via `tprobe2`

**What `tprobe2` needs** (minimum viable copy from `../iiac-perf/src`):

| iiac-perf file | actor-x1 destination | notes |
|---|---|---|
| `tprobe2.rs` | `src/tprobe2.rs` | unchanged |
| `band_table.rs` | `src/band_table.rs` | rewrite one `use` to point at our fmt module |
| `ticks.rs` | `src/ticks.rs` | unchanged |
| `ticks/x86_64.rs` | `src/ticks/x86_64.rs` | unchanged |
| `fmt_commas` + `fmt_commas_f64` (from `harness.rs` lines 162–188) | `src/fmt.rs` *(new, ~30 lines)* | extract just those two fns; skips harness's 393-line benchmark apparatus we don't need |

**Crate dependencies added to `Cargo.toml`**: `hdrhistogram = "7"`, `minstant = "0.1"`. Nothing else from iiac-perf's chain is required.

**Arch caveat**: tprobe2's tick counter is x86_64-only today — `ticks.rs` has a `compile_error!` on other arches. **The bot thinks** this is fine for us (your dev box is x86_64) but worth naming so it isn't a later surprise.

**Where the probe sites go in Goal1**
- `TProbe2` lives on the single-threaded runtime alongside the message queue.
- Wrap the dispatch call: `let id = probe.start(actor_id as u64); actor.handle_message(&mut ctx, msg); probe.end(id);` — `site_id = actor_id` so per-actor latency shows up separately if we add per-site grouping later.
- After the `Instant::now() >= deadline` loop exits, call `probe.report(false)` — prints the band table in ns alongside the existing message-count summary.
- Add `--ticks` flag (or `-t`) to flip to raw ticks, matching the iiac-perf CLI convention.

**Revised version ladder**
- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo + plan marker
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1 base (ping-pong + duration + count)
- `0.1.0-2` — copy `tprobe2` + wire probe sites into Goal1 (this is the added step)
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2
- `0.1.0` — final: todo/chores update, drop `-N` suffix

**Notes/chores entry for the copy**: a `## Vendored perf probe (0.1.0-2)` section in `chores-01.md` recording the source path (`../iiac-perf/src`), files copied, and the "promote to shared lib later" intent.

**Things to confirm before I start**
1. Scope granularity: **per-dispatch** (proposed above — one `start`/`end` per `handle_message` call) or **per-round-trip** (probe wraps one ping-pong, actor-pair latency)? Per-dispatch matches tprobe2's design more naturally; round-trip tells you end-to-end messaging cost.
2. Apply probe to **Goal2 too**? `TProbe2` is `Send` but not `Sync`, so each threaded actor would own its own probe and report on shutdown. Straightforward but adds surface area.
3. Module naming: `src/tprobe2.rs` at top level (flat, matches source) vs `src/perf/{tprobe2,band_table,ticks,fmt}.rs` (grouped, easier to extract to a lib later). I lean `src/perf/` for the grouping; happy either way.

"y" to which? I need a few specifics before starting — can you confirm:

1. **Handler signature** (from the first proposal, unresolved) — (a) return `Option<(u32, Message)>`, (b) `&mut self` + `Context::send`, or (c) keep literal spec?
2. **Probe scope** — per-dispatch or per-round-trip?
3. **Probe on Goal2 too?** — y/n
4. **Module layout** — flat (`src/tprobe2.rs`) or grouped (`src/perf/`)?

If you meant "yes to all my recommendations" that would be: **(b), per-dispatch, yes Goal2 too, `src/perf/`** — please confirm.

input.new_string:
## Stage 1 runtime — plan marker (0.1.0-0)

Begins the Stage 1 implementation described in [design.md](design.md).
Multi-step ladder (see [Versioning during development](../CLAUDE.md#versioning)):

- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo crate + this plan marker (no behavior).
- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1: two actors, single thread, ping-pong an empty
  `Message` for a caller-supplied duration in seconds. Reports
  total messages handled.
- `0.1.0-2` — vendor `tprobe2` (and its transitive deps: `band_table`,
  `ticks`, `fmt_commas*`) from `../iiac-perf/src` into `src/perf/`.
  Wrap per-dispatch `handle_message` calls with `probe.start`/`probe.end`
  using `site_id = actor_id`; print a band-table report at shutdown
  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
  `TProbe2`, report on join).
- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
  `notes/README.md`.

### Design decisions recorded here

- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
  to thread outbound sends through.
- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
  driving them.
- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
  restriction is documented at the vendored module.
